title: The Guardrail and the Doctor
summary: A patient triage assistant blocks nearly half of legitimate medical questions. The guardrail is doing its job too well.

resolution.root_cause: A Bedrock Guardrail's denied topic 'Medical Advice and Diagnosis' was configured with overly broad definition and example phrases that match any mention of medical symptoms, conditions, or procedures. Since the triage assistant's purpose is symptom collection, 40% of legitimate patient queries trigger the guardrail and receive a blocked response instead of reaching the foundation model.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Bedrock Guardrail is blocking legitimate patient queries via the denied topic filter
Player identifies the 'Medical Advice and Diagnosis' denied topic as too broadly defined for a medical triage use case
Player proposes narrowing the denied topic to block only specific prohibited actions (prescribing, diagnosing) rather than all medical terminology
Player notes the SNS topic for guardrail interventions has no subscribers, so nobody was alerted to the high intervention rate

learning_objectives: How Bedrock Guardrails denied topics evaluate and intercept user input before it reaches the foundation model
The importance of testing guardrail configurations against legitimate use-case queries, not just adversarial ones
Balancing AI safety controls with application functionality in domain-specific contexts
Setting up alerting on guardrail intervention rates to detect false-positive spikes
sop_steps: 1. Check the Lambda function's logs in CloudWatch Logs (AWS's centralized log viewer) to confirm that requests are being blocked before they ever reach the AI model. Look for BLOCKED responses in the guardrail output.

2. Pull up the guardrail's full configuration using the get_guardrail command. A guardrail is a safety filter that sits between users and the AI model. Look at all the denied topics -- subjects the guardrail is told to reject -- along with their definitions and example phrases.

3. Compare the current guardrail version (v3) against the previous one (v2) using get_guardrail_version. Identify what changed in the most recent update -- specifically, what new denied topic was added.

4. Read the guardrail trace, which is a diagnostic log showing exactly why a request was blocked. Confirm which denied topic is triggering and whether the block happens at the input stage (before the model sees the request) or the output stage (after the model responds).

5. Reproduce the problem by testing real patient queries -- symptom descriptions, medication questions -- against the guardrail using apply_guardrail. This confirms that legitimate queries are being incorrectly blocked (a false positive).

6. Fix the denied topic definition. Right now it blocks based on what the patient types in (input filtering). Change it to block based on what the model says back (output filtering). The new definition should target responses where the model renders a diagnosis or prescribes treatment, not patient descriptions of symptoms.

7. Replace the example phrases the guardrail uses for matching. Remove input-style phrases like 'chest pain' and 'shortness of breath' (which are exactly what patients need to type). Add output-style phrases like 'you likely have,' 'I recommend taking,' and 'your diagnosis is' (which are what the model should not say).

8. Flip the denied topic's action settings: change inputAction from BLOCK to NONE (let patient messages through) and outputAction from NONE to BLOCK (catch the model if it tries to diagnose or prescribe).

9. Create a new guardrail version with these changes and test it against two sets of queries: adversarial prompts (things that should be blocked) and legitimate patient queries (things that should pass through). Both sets must behave correctly.

10. Subscribe the on-call clinical engineering team to the SNS topic patchwork-guardrail-alerts. SNS (Simple Notification Service) is AWS's messaging system -- but an alert channel with zero subscribers is the same as having no alerts at all.

11. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the GuardrailInterventions metric (which counts how many times the guardrail blocks a request). Set a threshold of 50 per hour and connect it to the SNS alert topic so the team gets paged immediately if the guardrail starts blocking an unusual number of requests.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Content filter strength set too high blocks clinical language classified as violence.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this case the block comes from a different part of the guardrail -- the content filter, which scans for harmful categories like violence, hate, and sexual content. Descriptions of injuries or pain ('stabbing pain in my chest') can be misclassified as violent content when the VIOLENCE filter is set to HIGH sensitivity. The guardrail trace (the diagnostic log) would show a content policy intervention instead of a topic policy intervention, so it looks different from the denied-topic problem in this sim.
related_failure_modes.prevention: For healthcare applications where patients routinely describe injuries and pain, lower the VIOLENCE content filter from HIGH to MEDIUM. Always test with real clinical language before changing filter sensitivity levels.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Guardrail version rollback reverts to a version without the Insurance Fraud denied topic.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: This is the opposite problem -- the guardrail blocks too little instead of too much. If you roll back from version 3 all the way to version 1 to fix the overly broad Medical Advice topic, you also lose the legitimate Insurance Fraud denied topic that was added in version 2. The failure is silent: nobody gets alerted because the number of blocked requests goes down, not up. You would only discover the gap if someone tried to commit fraud through the assistant.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Never roll back to an older guardrail version without reviewing every denied topic it contains. Instead of rolling back, create a new version that removes only the problematic topic while keeping the others intact.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The denied topic definition is correct but the example phrases are too similar to each other, reducing detection accuracy.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: This is the mirror image of the current sim: instead of blocking too many legitimate requests (false positives), the guardrail fails to block requests it should catch (false negatives). The example phrases all say roughly the same thing, so the guardrail does not recognize different ways of expressing the same forbidden topic. The guardrail trace would show the topic as 'not detected' even when the message clearly matches the topic definition.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Write example phrases that cover distinct variations of the forbidden topic. AWS recommends providing up to five diverse examples. Test against both obvious violations and edge cases that should be caught.

sop_practices: Before deploying any guardrail change, test it against real user queries -- not just the tricky prompts you want to block. Keep a test suite of at least 20 actual patient queries alongside 10 adversarial prompts, and verify that the guardrail blocks the bad ones while letting the legitimate ones through.

Think carefully about whether a denied topic should filter the user's input, the model's output, or both. In specialized applications like medical triage, where users naturally type words that sound like the denied topic (symptom names, medication names), apply the topic filter only to the model's output. This lets patients describe their symptoms freely while still preventing the model from crossing into diagnosis or prescription.

Whenever you create an SNS alert topic (a notification channel in AWS's Simple Notification Service), subscribe at least one person or team to it right away. An alert topic with zero subscribers is the same as having no alerting at all -- events fire into the void and nobody finds out.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm (an automated alert rule) on the GuardrailInterventions metric, which counts how many requests the guardrail blocks. Base the threshold on your normal block rate. A sudden spike means a configuration change is catching content it should not be catching.

glossary.Bedrock Guardrail: A set of configurable safety filters that sit between the user and a foundation model. Guardrails evaluate text and can block requests or responses that match rules you define.
glossary.Denied Topic: A subject you tell the guardrail to reject. You provide a name, a short definition, and optional example phrases. If the guardrail detects the topic in text, it blocks that text.
glossary.Foundation Model: A large pre-trained AI model hosted by Amazon Bedrock. Your application sends prompts to it and receives generated text back. The model itself does not enforce safety rules -- the guardrail does.
glossary.Input Filtering: Evaluating the user's message before it reaches the foundation model. If the guardrail blocks at this stage, the model never sees the request at all.
glossary.Output Filtering: Evaluating the model's response before it reaches the user. The model processes the request, but the guardrail can still block the response if it violates policy.
glossary.Guardrail Trace: A diagnostic record that shows which filters evaluated the text, what was detected, and whether the guardrail intervened. Useful for understanding why a request was blocked.
glossary.Content Filter: A guardrail component that detects harmful content in categories like hate, violence, or sexual content. Each category has a configurable strength level from NONE to HIGH.
glossary.SNS Topic: Simple Notification Service topic. A messaging channel that can send alerts to email, SMS, or other endpoints. It only works if at least one endpoint is subscribed.
glossary.CloudWatch Metric: A time-series data point published by an AWS service. You can set alarms on metrics to get notified when values cross a threshold.
glossary.GuardrailInterventions: A CloudWatch metric that counts how many times a Bedrock Guardrail blocked a request or response. A sudden spike usually means a configuration change is catching unintended content.

components.name: Patient Mobile App
components.role: Frontend interface where patients type symptom descriptions to begin triage
components.failure_impact: None -- the app is functioning correctly

components.name: API Gateway
components.role: Routes HTTPS requests from the patient app to the Lambda function
components.failure_impact: None -- requests are arriving at the backend

components.name: Lambda Function (patchwork-triage-handler)
components.role: Receives patient queries, formats prompts, and calls Bedrock InvokeModel with the guardrail attached
components.failure_impact: None -- the function executes correctly and passes the query to Bedrock

components.name: Bedrock Guardrail (patchwork-clinical-safety v3)
components.role: Evaluates text against content filters and denied topics before and after model invocation
components.failure_impact: The denied topic 'Medical Advice and Diagnosis' blocks 40% of patient queries at the input stage. The foundation model never sees these requests. Patients receive a generic blocked message.

components.name: Foundation Model (Claude 3 Haiku)
components.role: Generates triage categorizations from patient symptom descriptions
components.failure_impact: None -- the model works correctly for the 60% of queries that reach it

components.name: SNS Topic (patchwork-guardrail-alerts)
components.role: Intended to deliver alerts when guardrail intervention rates spike
components.failure_impact: Zero subscribers. The spike in interventions went unnoticed for two days because nobody receives notifications from this topic.

components.name: CloudWatch Metrics
components.role: Records GuardrailInterventions count and other operational metrics
components.failure_impact: Metrics are recording correctly but no alarm is configured to act on the GuardrailInterventions spike

system.data_flow: Patient types a symptom description in the mobile app. The request goes through API Gateway to the Lambda function. Lambda calls Bedrock InvokeModel with the guardrail. The guardrail evaluates the user input against denied topics first. If the denied topic matches, the request is blocked before the foundation model sees it and the patient receives 'I'm sorry, I can't help with that request.' If no topic matches, the foundation model generates a triage categorization and returns it through Lambda to the patient.
system.what_broke: The guardrail's denied topic 'Medical Advice and Diagnosis' was configured with a broad definition and example phrases like 'chest pain,' 'shortness of breath,' and 'medication for.' These phrases appear in virtually every legitimate triage query. The topic was set to evaluate user input with inputAction BLOCK and outputAction NONE. This means patient symptom descriptions were blocked before reaching the model. The guardrail was doing exactly what it was configured to do -- the configuration was wrong for the use case. The SNS topic had zero subscribers, so the 200x spike in GuardrailInterventions (from ~2/day to ~440/day) went unnoticed for two days.

progressive_clues: responses say the request was blocked, not that the model could not answer -- something is intercepting requests before the model sees them
bedrock guardrails evaluate input before it reaches the model -- has the guardrail configuration changed recently? check the version history
look at the denied topics in the guardrail -- is there one whose example phrases might match legitimate medical triage queries like symptom descriptions?

the denied topic 'Medical Advice and Diagnosis' is defined too broadly -- its example phrases match any symptom description, which is exactly what the triage assistant collects. fix is to narrow the topic and move it to output filtering
